mechanism_narrative: When the Federal Funds effective rate exceeds 4.0%, monetary policy becomes restrictive, increasing the cost of capital for firms and depressing consumer credit. This tightening of financial conditions leads to lower corporate investment and valuations, causing negative equity returns over the following quarter.
evidence_refs: fact:Fed has been raising rates throughout 2005
failure_condition: The average 60-day forward return for the S&P 500 is not statistically significantly negative (p > 0.05) during periods when FEDFUNDS is above 4.0%.

mechanism_narrative: Sustained growth in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) is a direct signal of a robust and expanding economy. This strength translates into higher aggregate demand and corporate earnings, which supports positive equity market performance over the next month. This is an always-on signal reflecting underlying economic momentum.
evidence_refs: fact:PAYEMS has shown consistent growth through 2005
failure_condition: The rolling 36-month correlation between the 3-month change in PAYEMS and 20-day forward S&P 500 returns is negative or statistically insignificant.

mechanism_narrative: The combination of very high housing starts (HOUST) and a rising federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) creates a high-risk environment for equities. Aggressive monetary tightening will prick the housing bubble, leading to a sharp contraction in construction, consumer wealth, and related spending, triggering a broad market downturn.
evidence_refs: fact:Housing market is widely considered to be 'hot' or in a bubble in late 2005
failure_condition: S&P 500 produces positive 60-day returns in more than 50% of instances when the trigger condition is met.

mechanism_narrative: Positive surprises or acceleration in Industrial Production (INDPRO) reflect a strengthening supply side of the economy and robust business activity. Markets react quickly to this information, pricing in higher near-term earnings and driving positive returns over a short horizon.
failure_condition: The information coefficient of month-over-month INDPRO changes on 5-day forward S&P 500 returns is consistently below 0.02 in a walk-forward test.

mechanism_narrative: During a monetary tightening cycle, an acceleration in inflation (CPIAUCSL) forces the central bank to become more hawkish than anticipated. This increases the discount rate applied to future earnings and signals a higher risk of a policy-induced recession, leading to negative equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Energy prices have contributed to higher inflation in 2005
failure_condition: When the FEDFUNDS rate is rising, periods of accelerating CPI are not followed by statistically significant negative 20-day S&P 500 returns.

mechanism_narrative: When the unemployment rate (UNRATE) falls below 5.0%, the labor market is considered at or beyond full employment. This condition fuels wage-push inflation, compelling the Federal Reserve to maintain or accelerate monetary tightening, which is a headwind for equities over the medium term.
evidence_refs: fact:The unemployment rate has steadily declined to 5.0% by late 2005
failure_condition: The average 60-day S&P 500 return is positive during periods when the unemployment rate is below 5.0%.

mechanism_narrative: The momentum of retail and food services sales (RSAFS) is a high-frequency indicator of consumer health. A positive 3-month change indicates resilient consumer spending, which supports corporate revenues and drives positive equity returns over the subsequent month.
failure_condition: The rank information coefficient between the 3-month change in RSAFS and 20-day forward returns remains below 0.03 over any 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: A simultaneous rise in inflation (CPIAUCSL) and the unemployment rate (UNRATE) signals a stagflationary environment. This is unequivocally negative for equities, as it implies eroding corporate profit margins (from higher costs) and weakening demand, while constraining the central bank's ability to provide stimulus.
failure_condition: In a walk-forward test, triggered instances are not followed by negative 60-day S&P 500 returns in at least 60% of cases.
